my_sentence: I brought my daughter go to the shopping mall to buy something.
correction: I brought my daughter to the shopping mall.
explanation: 'bring someone somewhere' already carries the motion, so you don't add a second verb 'go to'. Use 'bring/take + person + to + place' directly.
correct_examples: I took my daughter to the shopping mall.
She brought her son to the park.
context: Talking about the weekend with his daughter

my_sentence: We need to send her to hospital to do some check up.
correction: We needed to send her to the hospital to do a check up.
explanation: Use 'the hospital' (a specific place) and 'a check up' (a single countable event). Also, since this was a past situation, the verb should be 'needed', not 'need'.
correct_examples: I took her to the hospital for a check up.
He went to the clinic for a quick check up.
context: Taking his daughter for a health check

my_sentence: There are too many students graduated in one year.
correction: There are too many students who graduate in the same year.
explanation: To modify 'students' with an action, use a relative clause 'who graduate', not the bare past participle 'graduated'. For a general yearly fact, use the present 'graduate', and 'in the same year' for the intended meaning.
correct_examples: There are too many students who graduate in the same year.
Many people who graduate each year cannot find a job.
context: Why college graduates struggle to find jobs

my_sentence: After they graduated from college, they can't find a suitable job.
correction: After they graduate from college, they can't find a suitable job.
explanation: For a general, repeated truth, both clauses stay in the present: 'After they graduate ... they can't find ...'. The past 'graduated' would only fit a specific past event.
correct_examples: After they graduate, many young people move to big cities.
After he finishes school, he looks for work.
context: General pattern of graduates and jobs

my_sentence: That means they have a terrible childhood.
correction: That means they had a terrible childhood.
explanation: Childhood is finished and in the past, so use the past tense 'had'. 'have' would wrongly suggest it is still happening now.
correct_examples: They had a difficult childhood.
She had a happy childhood in the countryside.
context: Children pushed too hard by parents

my_sentence: Children must need to learn 20 to 30 words this day.
correction: Children must learn 20 to 30 words.
explanation: Don't stack two modals/semi-modals. 'must' already means obligation, so 'must need to' is redundant; say 'must learn' or 'need to learn', not both.
correct_examples: Students must learn 20 new words a day.
They need to learn 20 new words a day.
context: Describing a strict parent's demands on a teacher

my_sentence: They get educated from 1978.
correction: They started getting education in 1978.
explanation: To mark the beginning of something at a point in time, use 'started + -ing' and the preposition 'in' for a year: 'started getting education in 1978'. 'get educated from 1978' is ungrammatical here.
correct_examples: Women started getting an education in 1978.
He started learning English in 2010.
context: When women in China began to be educated

my_sentence: Some people want to finish their life and try to suicide.
correction: Some people want to commit suicide.
explanation: 'suicide' is a noun, not a verb, and the fixed collocation is 'commit suicide'. You cannot say 'to suicide' or 'finish their life'.
correct_examples: Sadly, some students commit suicide from the pressure.
He was so depressed he thought about committing suicide.
context: Consequences of excessive academic pressure

my_sentence: We need to reduce some stress.
correction: We need to reduce stress. / We need to reduce being stressed out.
explanation: 'stress' here is uncountable and general, so drop 'some'. Native speakers say 'reduce stress' or, more colloquially, 'reduce being stressed out'.
correct_examples: We need to reduce stress on students.
Exercise helps reduce stress.
context: Lowering pressure on children

my_sentence: They find being an ordinary people is good.
correction: They find being an ordinary person is good.
explanation: 'an' is singular, so it must take the singular noun 'person'. 'people' is plural and cannot follow 'an'.
correct_examples: Being an ordinary person is enough.
He is just an ordinary person.
context: Parents accepting an ordinary life for their children

my_sentence: We need to reduce our expectation.
correction: We need to reduce our expectations.
explanation: 'expectation' is usually plural when talking about hopes in general: 'lower/reduce your expectations'.
correct_examples: We should lower our expectations.
Parents need to manage their expectations.
context: Parents lowering their hopes for their children

my_sentence: It is just to look ourself.
correction: It is just to look out for ourselves.
explanation: The phrasal verb 'look out for' means 'take care of / protect', and the reflexive pronoun must be plural 'ourselves' to match 'we'.
correct_examples: We just have to look out for ourselves.
Look out for yourself first.
context: Being realistic as ordinary people

my_sentence: Why our children need to be an elite?
correction: Why would our children need to become an elite?
explanation: A question needs an auxiliary and subject-auxiliary inversion: 'Why would our children ...?'. Also 'become an elite' fits the idea of turning into one better than 'be'.
correct_examples: Why would our children need to become elites?
Why do they have to be the best?
context: Questioning the pressure to make children elite

my_sentence: They do not think the results.
correction: They do not think about the results.
explanation: 'think' needs the preposition 'about' when it means 'consider': 'think about the results / consequences'.
correct_examples: They don't think about the consequences.
Think about your future.
context: Parents ignoring the consequences of pressure

my_sentence: I eat something before our classes.
correction: I ate something before our class.
explanation: The eating already happened before the class started, so use the past tense 'ate'.
correct_examples: I ate dinner before the meeting.
I had a snack before class.
context: Whether he had eaten before the lesson

my_sentence: We couldn't finish our homework tonight.
correction: We couldn't finish our homework at night.
explanation: 'tonight' means the night of today; for a general habit in the past use 'at night'. He was describing childhood evenings, not this evening.
correct_examples: We studied at night by candlelight.
He often works at night.
context: Childhood with no electricity

my_sentence: We need to do my homework until ten o'clock.
correction: We needed to do our homework until ten o'clock.
explanation: This was a past habit, so use 'needed'. Also keep the pronoun consistent: after 'we', use 'our homework', not 'my homework'.
correct_examples: We needed to do our homework until late at night.
We had to finish our homework by ten.
context: Heavy homework in middle and high school

my_sentence: Some adults can't thinking.
correction: Some adults can't think.
explanation: After a modal verb like 'can', use the bare base form 'think', not the '-ing' form.
correct_examples: Some people can't think for themselves.
He can't make decisions on his own.
context: Adults who only follow mainstream ideas

my_sentence: The government need to spend a lot of money to pay for that.
correction: The government needs to spend a lot of money to pay for it.
explanation: 'The government' is singular, so the verb takes -s: 'needs'. Also use 'pay for it' to refer back to a single thing already mentioned.
correct_examples: The government needs to invest in education.
The company needs to pay for it.
context: Cost of power failures to factories

my_sentence: If a child don't have enough education, he or she can't find a suitable job.
correction: If a child doesn't have enough education, he or she would not be able to find a suitable job.
explanation: 'a child' is singular, so use 'doesn't', not 'don't'. In this hypothetical, 'would not be able to find' is more natural than 'can't find'.
correct_examples: If a child doesn't get a good education, he or she will struggle later.
If a student doesn't study, he won't pass.
context: Consequences of neglecting a child's education

my_sentence: He or she needs to be take care of their parents.
correction: He or she needs to be taken care of by his or her parents.
explanation: In a passive form after 'be', use the past participle 'taken', not 'take'. The agent is introduced with 'by', and keep the pronoun consistent ('his or her', not 'their').
correct_examples: She needs to be taken care of by her family.
The patient must be taken care of by a nurse.
context: An uneducated person depending on family

my_sentence: I never been bullied.
correction: I was never bullied.
explanation: A passive past statement needs the verb 'be': 'I was never bullied'. 'I never been' is missing 'was' (or, with 'have', it would be 'I have never been bullied').
correct_examples: I was never bullied at school.
He has never been bullied.
context: Whether he was ever bullied as a child

my_sentence: The school reputation will go down.
correction: The school's reputation will go down.
explanation: To show that the reputation belongs to the school, use the possessive 's: 'the school's reputation'.
correct_examples: The school's reputation will suffer.
The company's image was damaged.
context: Schools that fail their students

my_sentence: That's a disaster for he or her family, too.
correction: That's a disaster for his or her family, too.
explanation: Before a noun you need the possessive 'his or her', not the subject pronoun 'he'. (assistant note: not explicitly corrected, but consistently wrong.)
correct_examples: It's a disaster for his or her family.
Each student should bring his or her book.
context: Effect of a ruined life on the family

my_sentence: They always break the red light.
correction: They always run a red light. / They always run red lights.
explanation: The natural collocation for ignoring a traffic light is 'run a red light', not 'break the red light'. (assistant note: not explicitly corrected in class.)
correct_examples: He ran a red light and almost caused an accident.
Don't run red lights.
context: Students who ignore traffic rules

my_sentence: They will have a happy life and pretty good friend.
correction: They can connect with some pretty good friends.
explanation: 'friend' should be plural 'friends', and the natural verb for making friends here is 'connect with', not 'have'.
correct_examples: They can make some good friends.
Kids connect with friends when they have free time.
context: Benefits of less homework for children
